source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure to Configure Security Groups

input_row.source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure to Configure Security Groups
input_row.description: Configuring security groups benefits the user because it helps manage networks within the database and gives only certain permission for traffic that leaves and enters the database.
input_row.rationale: Impact: Allows certain users to access the instance and it only allows them to work within that network.
input_row.audit: 1. Sign into the AWS Management Console • Sign into the AWS Management Console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/ with your AWS account credentials. 2. Open the Amazon RDS Console • Navigate to the service using the Find Services search bar or by directly accessing the console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/rds/. 3. Select the RDS Instance • Choose the Amazon RDS instance for which you want to configure security groups. Click on the instance name to access its details page. 4. Navigate to the Connectivity & Security Section • In the instance details page, navigate to the Connectivity & Security or "Security" section. 5. View and Modify Existing Security Groups • Under the Security section, you will see the existing security groups associated with the RDS instance. • Take note of the existing security groups and their inbound and outbound rules.   6. Create a New Security Group • If you need to create a new security group for the RDS instance • Click the Create New Security Group button. • Provide a name and description for the new security group. • Configure the inbound and outbound rules to control network traffic to and from the RDS instance. • Click "Create" to create the new security group. 7. Modify Security Group Rules • To modify the rules of an existing security group, click on the security group name or the Modify button next to it. • You can add, edit, or delete inbound and outbound rules on the security group details page. • Specify each rule's source IP addresses, port ranges, and protocols. • Click Save or Apply Changes to update the security group rules. 8. Associate Security Groups • To associate a security group with the RDS instance, navigate to the Connectivity & Security or Security section of the instance details page. • Click Modify next to the VPC security groups option. • Select the desired security groups from the list. • Click Save or Apply Changes to associate them with the RDS instance. 9. Verify and Test Security Group Configuration • Review the security group settings to match your network access requirements. • Test the connectivity to the RDS instance by attempting to access it from authorized IP addresses or applications. 10. Monitor and Update Security Groups • Regularly monitor the network traffic and access patterns to your RDS instance. • Update the security group rules as needed to reflect changes in your network access requirements.
input_row.impact: Allows certain users to access the instance and it only allows them to work within that network.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

- ✓ Can AWS APIs query this property? → Automated
- ✓ Is this a standard AWS service property (encryption, logging, access control, network rules)? → Automated
- ✓ Does boto3 SDK have methods to check this? → Automated
- ✓ Does AWS CLI have commands to retrieve this? → Automated
  
Configuring security groups for an RDS instance is a standard AWS function involving network rules, which are accessible through AWS APIs. The audit process primarily involves checking the security group rules associated with an RDS instance, which can be fully automated using AWS APIs like boto3 and AWS CLI.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_rds_instance_security_group_rules_verified

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

- **AWS API calls or boto3 SDK methods to use:**
  - Use `boto3.client('rds').describe_db_instances()` to iterate over all RDS instances.
  - Use `boto3.client('ec2').describe_security_groups()` to get details about each associated security group.

- **AWS CLI commands (if applicable):**
  - `aws rds describe-db-instances` to list RDS instances.
  - `aws ec2 describe-security-groups --group-ids <security-group-id>` to fetch security group details.

- **Specific resources to enumerate:**
  - All Amazon RDS instances and their associated VPC security groups across the AWS account and regions.

- **Exact configuration properties/settings to validate:**
  - Ensure security groups have rules configured according to best practices by checking:
    - Inbound rules: Ensure they only allow necessary and secure traffic.
    - Outbound rules: Ensure they are appropriately configured for the application requirements.

- **Clear Pass/Fail criteria with expected values:**
  - Pass: Security groups only allow expected traffic with specific source IP ranges and port/protocol specifications.
  - Fail: Any security group with overly broad or permissive rules, such as open to `0.0.0.0/0` or permitting unnecessary ports. 

By leveraging AWS APIs, an automated approach effectively validates that RDS instances are correctly secured at the network level through precise control of inbound and outbound traffic rules.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. Security group configurations can indeed be checked using AWS APIs, boto3, and AWS CLI. The use of these tools allows for efficient and comprehensive validation of security group rules associated with RDS instances.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly accurate. The use of `boto3.client('rds').describe_db_instances()` and `boto3.client('ec2').describe_security_groups()` is appropriate for retrieving RDS instances and their associated security group details.
- The AWS CLI commands `aws rds describe-db-instances` and `aws ec2 describe-security-groups --group-ids <security-group-id>` are valid and correctly specified for the task.
- The program name `aws_rds_instance_security_group_rules_verified` is somewhat descriptive but could be more specific about what is being checked (e.g., 'network_rules').
  
**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Program Name:** A more descriptive program name could be `aws_rds_security_group_network_rules_check` to clearly indicate the focus on network rules.
- **Additional Considerations:** Consider using AWS Config rules to continuously monitor security group configurations. AWS Config provides managed rules like `rds-instance-public-access-check` which can be leveraged for ongoing compliance.
- **Alternative Methods:** While the current methods are valid, using `boto3.resource('ec2').SecurityGroup(group_id)` can provide a more object-oriented approach to interacting with security group details.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded on AWS's capabilities and security best practices. The use of AWS APIs and tools is correctly identified and applied.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Modify:** Update the program name to `aws_rds_security_group_network_rules_check` for better clarity and descriptiveness.
- Consider integrating AWS Config for continuous monitoring and compliance checks, which can complement the automated audit process.

Overall, the audit assessment is technically sound, and the automation approach is appropriate for the control being evaluated.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that security group configurations for Amazon RDS instances are fully accessible via AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI), and all relevant properties (inbound/outbound rules, group associations) can be programmatically retrieved and evaluated. The audit steps described in the control can be translated into API calls without requiring any manual console inspection. The second opinion's suggestion for a more descriptive program name is valid and will be adopted for clarity.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
aws_rds_instance_security_group_network_rules_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- AWS Resource/Service: Amazon RDS Instances and associated EC2 Security Groups
- API/CLI Method:
  - `boto3.client('rds').describe_db_instances()` to enumerate all RDS instances and their associated VPC security group IDs.
  - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_security_groups(GroupIds=[...])` to retrieve the details of each security group.
- Properties to Check:
  - For each RDS instance, verify that at least one security group is attached.
  - For each security group, inspect `IpPermissions` (inbound rules) and `IpPermissionsEgress` (outbound rules) for:
    - Presence of overly permissive rules (e.g., 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0 for sensitive ports)
    - Alignment with organizational best practices (e.g., restrict access to known IP ranges, only required ports open)
- Pass Criteria:
  - Every RDS instance has at least one security group attached.
  - No security group attached to an RDS instance allows unrestricted inbound access (0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0) on sensitive database ports (e.g., 3306, 5432, 1433, etc.).
  - Security group rules follow least privilege and organizational policy.
- Fail Criteria:
  - Any RDS instance without a security group.
  - Any attached security group with overly permissive inbound rules (e.g., open to the world on database ports).
  - Security group rules that do not align with defined best practices.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agreed on the automated approach. The only minor difference was the specificity of the program name; the more descriptive suggestion from the second opinion was adopted for clarity.

**SOURCE:** CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
